surface_reasoning: Priority 3: labeled `module: performance`. Title reports `torch.gather` backward performance is extremely low specifically on `torch.bfloat16` — dtype-specific kernel dispatch signal indicating a missing or broken fast path for bfloat16.
blocking_signals: stale_no_activity: ~14 months open (Mar 2024 - Apr 2026), last maintainer comment never
confidence_justification: Start: 0.50. Step 1: triaged label present → +0.10. Running total: 0.60. Step 2: Repro script present — complete self-contained Python profiler script with quantified timing (fp32 bw 0.266ms vs bf16 bw 8.724ms on A100) → +0.15. Running total: 0.75. Step 3: fix_scope is single_function (scatter/gather backward kernel dispatch for bfloat16) → +0.10. Running total: 0.85. Step 4: stale_no_activity — 14 months open with zero maintainer comments → -0.05. Running total: 0.80. Step 5: No correctness (silent) label → no +0.25. Step 6: No maintainer signals (approved_fix, requested_merge) → no +0.40. Step 7: No wont_fix or blocked_action signals → no negative penalty. Step 8: Issue is under 2 years old (Mar 2024) → no +0.05. Step 9: No linked PRs — no prior fix attempt at all. Final: 0.80. Adjusted downward: zero maintainer engagement despite 14 months makes actionability uncertain — it's possible this is a known limitation of the bfloat16 scatter/gather backward kernel or was triaged without comment. The complete absence of any maintainer response after triage reduces confidence. Adjusted final: 0.60.
ground_truth_delta: Shares the positive_71774 profile in being a dtype-specific, narrowly-scoped performance bug with a repro script. Key differences: (1) no maintainer comments at all — the issue was triaged (labeled) but never commented on, unlike positive_71774 where COLLABORATORs confirmed the root cause; (2) no linked PR whatsoever; (3) it is a performance issue (missing fast path for bfloat16 backward) rather than a silent correctness bug, so it lacks the highest-priority correctness signal; (4) the repro script uses A100-specific hardware. The combination of zero maintainer discussion and a performance (not correctness) classification makes this less actionable than positive_71774, though the clear dtype-specific regression pattern is promising.
